title: Petroleum

infobox.value: or simply **oil**, is a naturally occurring, yellowish-black liquid [chemical mixture](/wiki/Chemical_mixture "Chemical mixture") found in [geological formations](/wiki/Geological_formation "Geological formation"), consisting mainly of [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The term *petroleum* refers both to naturally occurring unprocessed crude oil, as well as to [petroleum products](/wiki/Petroleum_product "Petroleum product") that consist of [refined](/wiki/Refining "Refining") crude oil.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Naturally occurring combustible liquid

"Crude oil" redirects here. For the 2008 film, see [*Crude Oil* (film)](/wiki/Crude_Oil_(film) "Crude Oil (film)"). For other uses, see [Petroleum (disambiguation)](/wiki/Petroleum_(disambiguation) "Petroleum (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Petroleum_sample.jpg/250px-Petroleum_sample.jpg)](/wiki/File:Petroleum_sample.jpg)

A sample of petroleum

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Oil_well.jpg/250px-Oil_well.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oil_well.jpg)

[Pumpjack](/wiki/Pumpjack "Pumpjack") pumping an oil well near [Lubbock, Texas](/wiki/Lubbock,_Texas "Lubbock, Texas"), U.S.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Photo_lg_kuwait.jpg/250px-Photo_lg_kuwait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photo_lg_kuwait.jpg)

An oil refinery in [Ahmadi Governorate](/wiki/Ahmadi_Governorate "Ahmadi Governorate") in Kuwait

**Petroleum**, also known as **crude oil** or simply **oil**, is a naturally occurring, yellowish-black liquid [chemical mixture](/wiki/Chemical_mixture "Chemical mixture") found in [geological formations](/wiki/Geological_formation "Geological formation"), consisting mainly of [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The term *petroleum* refers both to naturally occurring unprocessed crude oil, as well as to [petroleum products](/wiki/Petroleum_product "Petroleum product") that consist of [refined](/wiki/Refining "Refining") crude oil.

Petroleum is a [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") formed over millions of years from [anaerobic decay](/wiki/Anaerobic_decay "Anaerobic decay") of [organic materials](/wiki/Organic_material "Organic material") from buried [prehistoric](/wiki/Prehistoric_life "Prehistoric life") [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism"), particularly [planktons](/wiki/Plankton "Plankton") and [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae"). It is estimated that 70% of the world's oil deposits were formed during the [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic"), 20% were formed in the [Cenozoic](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic"), and only 10% were formed in the [Paleozoic](/wiki/Paleozoic "Paleozoic").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Conventional reserves of petroleum are primarily recovered by [drilling](/wiki/Oil_drilling "Oil drilling"), which is done after a study of the relevant [structural geology](/wiki/Structural_geology "Structural geology"), [analysis of the sedimentary basin](/wiki/Sedimentary_basin_analysis "Sedimentary basin analysis"), and [characterization of the petroleum reservoir](/wiki/Reservoir_characterization "Reservoir characterization"). There are also [unconventional](/wiki/Unconventional_(oil_%26_gas)_reservoir "Unconventional (oil & gas) reservoir") reserves such as [oil sands](/wiki/Oil_sands "Oil sands") and [oil shale](/wiki/Oil_shale "Oil shale") which are recovered by other means such as [fracking](/wiki/Fracking "Fracking").

Once extracted, oil is refined and separated, most easily by [distillation](/wiki/Continuous_distillation#Continuous_distillation_of_crude_oil "Continuous distillation"), into innumerable products for direct use or use in manufacturing. Petroleum products include fuels such as [gasoline](/wiki/Gasoline "Gasoline") (petrol), [diesel](/wiki/Diesel_fuel "Diesel fuel"), [kerosene](/wiki/Kerosene "Kerosene") and [jet fuel](/wiki/Jet_fuel "Jet fuel"); [bitumen](/wiki/Bitumen "Bitumen"), [paraffin wax](/wiki/Paraffin_wax "Paraffin wax") and lubricants; [reagents](/wiki/Reagent "Reagent") used to make [plastics](/wiki/Plastic "Plastic"); [solvents](/wiki/Solvents "Solvents"), textiles, [refrigerants](/wiki/Refrigerants "Refrigerants"), paint, [synthetic rubber](/wiki/Synthetic_rubber "Synthetic rubber"), [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizers "Fertilizers"), [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide"), pharmaceuticals, and thousands of other [petrochemicals](/wiki/Petrochemical "Petrochemical"). Petroleum is used in manufacturing a vast variety of materials essential for modern life,[[3]](#cite_note-Dixie_State_College_2-3) and it is estimated that the world consumes about 100 million [barrels](/wiki/Barrel_(unit)#Oil_barrel "Barrel (unit)") (16 million [cubic metres](/wiki/Cubic_metre "Cubic metre")) each day. Petroleum production played a key role in industrialization and [economic development](/wiki/Economic_development "Economic development"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) especially after the [Second Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Second_Industrial_Revolution "Second Industrial Revolution"). Some petroleum-rich countries, known as [petrostates](/wiki/Petrostate "Petrostate"), gained significant economic and international influence during the latter half of the 20th century due to their control of oil production and trade.

Petroleum is a [non-renewable resource](/wiki/Non-renewable_resource "Non-renewable resource"), and its [exploitation](/wiki/Exploitation_of_natural_resources "Exploitation of natural resources") is damaging to the [natural environment](/wiki/Natural_environment "Natural environment"), [climate system](/wiki/Climate_system "Climate system") and human health. [Extraction](/wiki/Extraction_of_petroleum "Extraction of petroleum"), [refining](/wiki/Petroleum_industry "Petroleum industry") and burning of petroleum fuels counteract [carbon sinks](/wiki/Carbon_sink "Carbon sink") by [releasing](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") large quantities of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") back into the Earth's atmosphere, making petroleum a major contributor to [anthropogenic climate change](/wiki/Anthropogenic_climate_change "Anthropogenic climate change"). Other [negative environmental effects](/wiki/Health_and_environmental_impact_of_the_petroleum_industry "Health and environmental impact of the petroleum industry"), at almost all stages of use, include direct release—such as [oil spills](/wiki/Oil_spill "Oil spill")—and secondary pollution of air and water sources.

Proximity to petroleum deposits, and subsequent access to and pricing of oil have historically fueled both domestic and geopolitical conflicts, state-sanctioned [oil wars](/wiki/Oil_war "Oil war"), diplomatic and [trade frictions](/wiki/Trade_war "Trade war"), [energy policy](/wiki/Energy_policy "Energy policy") disputes and other [resource conflicts](/wiki/Environmental_conflict "Environmental conflict"). While production is estimated to reach [peak oil](/wiki/Peak_oil "Peak oil") before 2035,[[5]](#cite_note-5) global economic focus on [climate change mitigation](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") in the transition to [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") sources and increased electrification will greatly reduce dependency on petroleum.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Treibs%26Chlorophyll.png/500px-Treibs%26Chlorophyll.png)](/wiki/File:Treibs%26Chlorophyll.png)

The close structural similarity of vanadium [porphyrin](/wiki/Porphyrin "Porphyrin") compound (left) extracted from petroleum and [chlorophyll a](/wiki/Chlorophyll_a "Chlorophyll a") (right) established the biological origin of petroleum.

The word *petroleum* comes from Medieval Latin *[petroleum](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/petroleum#Latin "wikt:petroleum")* (literally 'rock oil'), which comes from Latin [petra](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/petra#Latin "wikt:petra") 'rock' (from Greek *pétra* [πέτρα](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%80%CE%AD%CF%84%CF%81%CE%B1#Ancient_Greek "wikt:πέτρα")) and [oleum](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/oleum#Latin "wikt:oleum") 'oil' (from Greek *élaion* [ἔλαιον](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%94%CE%BB%CE%B1%CE%B9%CE%BF%CE%BD#Ancient_Greek "wikt:ἔλαιον")).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The origin of the term stems from monasteries in southern Italy where it was in use by the end of the first millennium as an alternative for the older term "[naphtha](/wiki/Naphtha "Naphtha")".[[9]](#cite_note-van_Dijk_2022,_n-9) After that, the term was used in numerous manuscripts and books, such as in the treatise *[De Natura Fossilium](/wiki/De_Natura_Fossilium "De Natura Fossilium")*, published in 1546 by German mineralogist [Georg Bauer](/wiki/Georg_Bauer "Georg Bauer").[[10]](#cite_note-10) After the advent of the oil industry during the second half of the 19th century, the term became commonly known for the liquid form of hydrocarbons.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the petroleum industry](/wiki/History_of_the_petroleum_industry "History of the petroleum industry")

### Early

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Gusher_Okemah_OK_1922.jpg/250px-Gusher_Okemah_OK_1922.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gusher_Okemah_OK_1922.jpg)

An oil derrick in [Okemah, Oklahoma](/wiki/Okemah,_Oklahoma "Okemah, Oklahoma") in 1922

Petroleum in one form or another has been used since ancient times. More than 4,300 years ago, [bitumen](/wiki/Bitumen "Bitumen") was mentioned when the [Sumerians](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") used it to make boats. A tablet of the legend of the birth of [Sargon of Akkad](/wiki/Sargon_of_Akkad "Sargon of Akkad") mentions a basket which was closed by straw and bitumen. More than 4,000 years ago, according to [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") and [Diodorus Siculus](/wiki/Diodorus_Siculus "Diodorus Siculus"), asphalt (bitumen) was used in the construction of the walls and towers of [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon"); there were oil pits near [Ardericca](/wiki/Ardericca_in_Susiana "Ardericca in Susiana") and Babylon and a pitch spring on [Zakynthos](/wiki/Zakynthos "Zakynthos").[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11) In [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon"), petroleum was used for road construction, ship caulking, and medicine.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The use of petroleum in ancient China dates back more than 2,000 years. The *[I Ching](/wiki/I_Ching "I Ching")*, one of the earliest Chinese writings, cites that oil in its raw state, without refining, was first discovered, extracted, and used in China in the 1st century BCE. In addition, the Chinese were the first to record the use of petroleum as fuel as early as the 4th century BCE.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) By 347 CE, oil was produced from bamboo-drilled wells in China.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In the 7th century, petroleum was among the essential ingredients for [Greek fire](/wiki/Greek_fire "Greek fire"), an incendiary projectile weapon that was used by [Byzantine Greeks](/wiki/Byzantine_Greeks "Byzantine Greeks") against Arab ships attacking [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Crude oil was distilled by [Persian chemists](/wiki/Alchemy_in_the_medieval_Islamic_world "Alchemy in the medieval Islamic world"), with clear descriptions given in Arabic handbooks such as those of [Abu Bakr al-Razi](/wiki/Abu_Bakr_al-Razi "Abu Bakr al-Razi").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In the 9th century, [oil fields](/wiki/Petroleum_reservoir "Petroleum reservoir") were exploited in the area around modern [Baku](/wiki/Baku "Baku"), Azerbaijan. These fields were described by Abu Bakr al-Razi in the 10th century and by [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo") in the 13th century, who describes the output of those wells as hundreds of shiploads.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Arab and Persian chemists distilled crude oil to produce flammable products for military purposes. Through [Islamic Spain](/wiki/Islamic_Spain "Islamic Spain"), distillation became available in Western Europe by the 12th century.[[21]](#cite_note-21) It was present in Romania since the 13th century, being recorded as păcură.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Sophisticated oil pits, 4.5 to 6 metres (15 to 20 ft) deep, were dug by the [Seneca people](/wiki/Seneca_people "Seneca people") and other [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") in [Western Pennsylvania](/wiki/Western_Pennsylvania "Western Pennsylvania") as early as 1415–1450. The French General [Louis-Joseph de Montcalm](/wiki/Louis-Joseph_de_Montcalm "Louis-Joseph de Montcalm") encountered Seneca using petroleum for ceremonial fires and as a healing lotion during a visit to [Fort Duquesne](/wiki/Fort_Duquesne "Fort Duquesne") in 1750.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Early British explorers to [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") documented a flourishing oil extraction industry based in [Yenangyaung](/wiki/Yenangyaung "Yenangyaung") that, in 1795, had hundreds of hand-dug wells under production.[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Merkwiller-Pechelbronn](/wiki/Merkwiller-Pechelbronn "Merkwiller-Pechelbronn") is said to be the first European site where petroleum has been explored and used. The still active Erdpechquelle, a spring where petroleum appears mixed with water, has been used since 1498, notably for medical purposes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Ignacy_Lukasiewicz.jpg/250px-Ignacy_Lukasiewicz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ignacy_Lukasiewicz.jpg)

[Ignacy Łukasiewicz](/wiki/Ignacy_%C5%81ukasiewicz "Ignacy Łukasiewicz"), credited with the first commercial oil extraction in 1854 in [Bóbrka](/wiki/B%C3%B3brka,_Krosno_County "Bóbrka, Krosno County"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), and opening the world's first industrial oil refinery.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Edwindrake.jpg/250px-Edwindrake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edwindrake.jpg)

In 1859, [Edwin Drake](/wiki/Edwin_Drake "Edwin Drake") drilled the world's first successful oil well at what is now known as [Drake Well](/wiki/Drake_Well "Drake Well") in [Cherrytree Township, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Cherrytree_Township,_Pennsylvania "Cherrytree Township, Pennsylvania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/West_Lothian_shale_bing%2C_Scotland.JPG/250px-West_Lothian_shale_bing%2C_Scotland.JPG)](/wiki/File:West_Lothian_shale_bing,_Scotland.JPG)

Shale [bings](/wiki/Spoil_tip "Spoil tip") near [Broxburn](/wiki/Broxburn "Broxburn"), three of a total of 19 in [West Lothian](/wiki/West_Lothian "West Lothian"), Scotland

In the mid-19th century, oil wells developed quickly in various parts of the world, though the title of the "first oil well" depends on the criteria. In 1846, a group of [Russian Imperial](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") engineers directed by Major Alexeyev of the Bakinskii Corps of Mining Engineers accidentally struck oil while hand-drilling with a primitive percussion rig in [Bibi-Heybat](/wiki/Bibiheyb%C9%99t "Bibiheybət"), near [Baku](/wiki/Baku "Baku") (now Azerbaijan), though they were not specifically searching for oil.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In 1853, [Ignacy Łukasiewicz](/wiki/Ignacy_%C5%81ukasiewicz "Ignacy Łukasiewicz"), who discovered how to distill [kerosene](/wiki/Kerosene "Kerosene") from seep crude oil and invented the modern [kerosene lamp](/wiki/Kerosene_lamp "Kerosene lamp"), hand-dug the first intentional well for commercial oil extraction in [Bóbrka](/wiki/B%C3%B3brka,_Krosno_County "Bóbrka, Krosno County"), Poland, to supply fuel for lighting (still operational as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit)).[[26]](#cite_note-:0-26) A hand-dug well and another refinery followed in 1857 near [Ploiești](/wiki/Ploie%C8%99ti "Ploiești"), Romania. Romania (then a vassal of the Ottoman Empire) was the first country in the world to have its annual crude oil output officially recorded in international statistics – 275 tonnes for 1857.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In 1858, Georg Christian Konrad Hunäus found a significant amount of petroleum while drilling for [lignite](/wiki/Lignite "Lignite") in [Wietze](/wiki/Wietze "Wietze"), Germany. Wietze later provided about 80% of German consumption in the Wilhelmine Era.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The production stopped in 1963, but Wietze has hosted a petroleum museum since 1970.[[30]](#cite_note-30) [Oil sands](/wiki/Oil_sands "Oil sands") have been mined since the 18th century.[[31]](#cite_note-31) In Wietze, natural asphalt/bitumen has been explored since the 18th century.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Both in Pechelbronn as in Wietze, the coal industry dominated the petroleum technologies.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Chemist [James Young](/wiki/James_Young_(chemist) "James Young (chemist)") in 1847 noticed a natural petroleum seepage in the coal mine at [Riddings](/wiki/Riddings "Riddings"), Derbyshire, from which he distilled a light thin oil suitable for use as lamp oil, at the same time obtaining a more viscous oil suitable for lubricating machinery. In 1848, Young set up a small business refining crude oil.[[34]](#cite_note-russell-34) Young eventually succeeded (by distilling [cannel coal](/wiki/Cannel_coal "Cannel coal") at low heat) in creating a fluid resembling petroleum, which when treated in the same way as the seep oil gave similar products. Young found that by slow distillation he could obtain several useful liquids from it, one of which he named "paraffine oil" because at low temperatures it congealed into a substance resembling [paraffin wax](/wiki/Paraffin_wax "Paraffin wax").[[34]](#cite_note-russell-34) The production of these oils and solid paraffin wax from coal formed the subject of his patent dated October 17, 1850. In 1850, Young & Meldrum and Edward William Binney entered into partnership under the title of E.W. Binney & Co. at [Bathgate](/wiki/Bathgate "Bathgate") in West Lothian and E. Meldrum & Co. at Glasgow; their works at Bathgate were completed in 1851 and became the first truly commercial oil-works in the world with the first modern oil refinery.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

The demand for petroleum as a fuel for lighting in North America and around the world quickly grew.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The first oil well in the Americas was drilled in 1859 by [Edwin Drake](/wiki/Edwin_Drake "Edwin Drake") at what is now called the [Drake Well](/wiki/Drake_Well "Drake Well") in [Cherrytree Township, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Cherrytree_Township,_Pennsylvania "Cherrytree Township, Pennsylvania"). There also was a company associated with it, and it sparked an [oil boom](/wiki/Oil_boom "Oil boom") and rapid expansion of the global petroleum industry. The same year, engine-drilled wells appeared in West Virginia.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The [first commercial oil well](/wiki/History_of_the_petroleum_industry_in_Canada#Early_origins "History of the petroleum industry in Canada") in Canada became operational in 1858 at [Oil Springs, Ontario](/wiki/Oil_Springs,_Ontario "Oil Springs, Ontario").[[39]](#cite_note-lclmg.org-39) Businessman [James Miller Williams](/wiki/James_Miller_Williams "James Miller Williams") dug several wells between 1855 and 1858 before discovering a rich reserve of oil four metres below ground.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[*[specify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Williams extracted 1.5 million litres of crude oil by 1860, refining much of it into kerosene lamp oil. Williams's well became commercially viable a year before Drake's Pennsylvania operation and could be argued to be the first commercial oil well in North America.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The discovery at Oil Springs touched off an oil boom which brought hundreds of speculators and workers to the area. Advances in drilling continued into 1862 when local driller Shaw reached a depth of 62 metres using the spring-pole drilling method.[[42]](#cite_note-42) On January 16, 1862, after an explosion of natural gas, Canada's first [oil gusher](/wiki/Blowout_(well_drilling) "Blowout (well drilling)") came into production, shooting into the air at a recorded rate of 480 cubic metres (3,000 bbl) per day.[[43]](#cite_note-43) By the end of the 19th century the Russian Empire, particularly the [Branobel](/wiki/Branobel "Branobel") company in Azerbaijan, had taken the lead in production.[[44]](#cite_note-Akiner-44)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Ride_with_hitler.jpg/250px-Ride_with_hitler.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ride_with_hitler.jpg)

A [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") poster promoting [carpooling](/wiki/Carpooling "Carpooling") as a way to ration vital gasoline during the war

In the 1930s, [Alfred Treibs](/wiki/Alfred_Treibs "Alfred Treibs") determined the biological origin of petroleum, which was previously controversial.[[45]](#cite_note-Treibs_1936_Chlorophyll-_und_Häminderivate-45)[[46]](#cite_note-Kvenvolden_2006_retrospective-46)

Access to oil was a major factor in several military conflicts of the 20th century, including [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), during which oil facilities were a major strategic asset and were [extensively bombed](/wiki/Oil_campaign_chronology_of_World_War_II "Oil campaign chronology of World War II").[[47]](#cite_note-47) The [German invasion of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") included the goal to capture the [Baku oilfields](/wiki/Petroleum_industry_in_Azerbaijan "Petroleum industry in Azerbaijan"), as it would provide much-needed oil supplies for the German military which was suffering from blockades.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[Oil exploration](/wiki/Oil_exploration "Oil exploration") in North America during the early 20th century led to the U.S. becoming the leading producer by mid-century. As petroleum production in the U.S. peaked during the 1960s, the United States was surpassed by Saudi Arabia and the Soviet Union in total output.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

During the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis"), Saudi Arabia and other [Arab nations](/wiki/Organization_of_Arab_Petroleum_Exporting_Countries "Organization of Arab Petroleum Exporting Countries") imposed an [oil embargo](/wiki/Oil_embargo "Oil embargo") against the United States, the United Kingdom, Japan and other Western nations which supported Israel in the [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War").[[52]](#cite_note-52) This was followed by the [1979 oil crisis](/wiki/1979_oil_crisis "1979 oil crisis"), which was caused by a drop in oil production in the wake of the [Iranian Revolution](/wiki/Iranian_Revolution "Iranian Revolution") and caused oil prices to more than double. The two oil price shocks had many short and long-term effects on global politics and the global economy.[[53]](#cite_note-53) They led to sustained reductions in demand as a result of substitution to other fuels, especially coal and nuclear, and improvements in [energy efficiency](/wiki/Efficient_energy_use "Efficient energy use"), facilitated by government policies.[[54]](#cite_note-54) High oil prices also induced investment in oil production by non-OPEC countries, including [Prudhoe Bay](/wiki/Prudhoe_Bay,_Alaska "Prudhoe Bay, Alaska") in Alaska, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") offshore fields of the United Kingdom and Norway, the Cantarell offshore field of Mexico, and oil sands in Canada.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 21st century")]

About 90 percent of vehicular fuel needs are met by oil.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Petroleum makes up 40 percent of total energy consumption in the United States, but is responsible for only one percent of electricity generation.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Petroleum's worth as a portable, dense energy source powering the vast majority of vehicles and as the base of many industrial chemicals makes it one of the world's most important [commodities](/wiki/Commodities "Commodities"). The top three oil-producing countries as of 2018 are the United States, Russia, and Saudi Arabia.[[57]](#cite_note-U.S._Energy_Information_Administration-57) In 2018, due in part to developments in [hydraulic fracturing](/wiki/Fracking "Fracking") and [horizontal drilling](/wiki/Directional_drilling "Directional drilling"), the United States became the world's largest producer.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

About 80 percent of the world's readily accessible reserves are located in the Middle East, with 62.5 percent coming from the Arab five: Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Iraq, Qatar, and Kuwait. A large portion of the world's total oil exists as unconventional sources, such as bitumen in [Athabasca oil sands](/wiki/Athabasca_oil_sands "Athabasca oil sands") and [extra heavy oil](/wiki/Heavy_crude_oil "Heavy crude oil") in the [Orinoco Belt](/wiki/Orinoco_Belt "Orinoco Belt"). While significant volumes of oil are extracted from oil sands, particularly in Canada, logistical and technical hurdles remain, as oil extraction requires large amounts of heat and water, making its net energy content quite low relative to conventional crude oil. Thus, Canada's oil sands are not expected to provide more than a few million barrels per day in the foreseeable future.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Many modern geopolitical conflicts are to due with petroleum.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

## Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Composition")]

Petroleum consists of a variety of liquid, gaseous, and solid components. Lighter [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") are the gases [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane"), [ethane](/wiki/Ethane "Ethane"), [propane](/wiki/Propane "Propane") and [butane](/wiki/Butane "Butane"). Otherwise the bulk of the liquid and solids are largely heavier [organic compounds](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound"), often hydrocarbons (C and H only). The proportion of light hydrocarbons in a petroleum mixture varies among oil fields.[[63]](#cite_note-Norman-2001-63)

An oil well produces predominantly crude oil. Because the pressure is lower at the surface than underground, some of the gas will come out of [solution](/wiki/Solution_(chemistry) "Solution (chemistry)") and be recovered (or burned) as *associated gas* or *solution gas*. A [gas well](/wiki/Gas_well "Gas well") produces predominantly [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"). However, because the underground temperature is higher than at the surface, the gas may contain heavier hydrocarbons such as [pentane](/wiki/Pentane "Pentane"), [hexane](/wiki/Hexane "Hexane"), and [heptane](/wiki/Heptane "Heptane") ("[natural-gas condensate](/wiki/Natural-gas_condensate "Natural-gas condensate")", often shortened to *condensate.*) Condensate resembles gasoline in appearance and is similar in composition to some [volatile](/wiki/Volatility_(chemistry) "Volatility (chemistry)") [light crude oils](/wiki/Light_crude_oil "Light crude oil").[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The hydrocarbons in crude oil are mostly [alkanes](/wiki/Alkane "Alkane"), [cycloalkanes](/wiki/Cycloalkane "Cycloalkane") and various [aromatic hydrocarbons](/wiki/Aromatic_compound "Aromatic compound"), while the other organic compounds contain [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen"), [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen"), and [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur"), and traces of metals such as [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"), [nickel](/wiki/Nickel "Nickel"), [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") and [vanadium](/wiki/Vanadium "Vanadium"). Many oil reservoirs contain live bacteria.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The molecular composition of crude oil varies widely from formation to formation, but the proportion of [chemical elements](/wiki/Chemical_element "Chemical element") varies over fairly narrow limits as follows:[[67]](#cite_note-Speight-1999-67)

Composition by weight

| Element | Percent range |
| --- | --- |
| [Carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") | 83 to 85% |
| [Hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") | 10 to 14% |
| [Nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") | 0.1 to 2% |
| [Oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") | 0.05 to 1.5% |
| [Sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") | 0.05 to 6.0% |
| Metals | < 0.1% |

Four different types of hydrocarbon appear in crude oil. The relative percentage of each varies, determining the properties of each oil.[[63]](#cite_note-Norman-2001-63)

Composition by weight

| Hydrocarbon | Average | Range |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Alkanes](/wiki/Alkane "Alkane") (paraffins) | 30% | 15 to 60% |
| [Naphthenes](/wiki/Naphthene "Naphthene") | 49% | 30 to 60% |
| [Aromatics](/wiki/Aromatic "Aromatic") | 15% | 3 to 30% |
| [Asphaltics](/wiki/Asphaltene "Asphaltene") | 6% | remainder |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Total_World_Oil_Reserves_Conventional_Unconventional.png/250px-Total_World_Oil_Reserves_Conventional_Unconventional.png)](/wiki/File:Total_World_Oil_Reserves_Conventional_Unconventional.png)

Unconventional resources are much larger than conventional ones.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Isooctane-3D-balls.png/250px-Isooctane-3D-balls.png)](/wiki/File:Isooctane-3D-balls.png)

[2,2,4-Trimethylpentane](/wiki/2,2,4-Trimethylpentane "2,2,4-Trimethylpentane"), a [hydrocarbon](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") with the [octane number](/wiki/Octane_number "Octane number") of 100. Black spheres are [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") and white spheres are [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") atoms.

The alkanes from pentane (C5H12) to octane (C8H18) are refined into gasoline, the ones from [nonane](/wiki/Nonane "Nonane") (C9H20) to [hexadecane](/wiki/Hexadecane "Hexadecane") (C16H34) into [diesel fuel](/wiki/Diesel_fuel "Diesel fuel"), kerosene and [jet fuel](/wiki/Jet_fuel "Jet fuel"). Alkanes with more than 16 carbon atoms can be refined into [fuel oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") and [lubricating oil](/wiki/Lubricating_oil "Lubricating oil"). At the heavier end of the range, paraffin wax is an alkane with approximately 25 carbon atoms, while asphalt has 35 and up, although these are usually [cracked](/wiki/Fluid_catalytic_cracking "Fluid catalytic cracking") in modern refineries into more valuable products. The lightest fraction, the so-called petroleum gases, are subjected to diverse processing depending on cost. These gases are either [flared off](/wiki/Gas_flare "Gas flare"), sold as [liquefied petroleum gas](/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas "Liquefied petroleum gas"), or used to power the refinery's own burners. During the winter, butane (C4H10) is blended into the gasoline pool at high rates because its high [vapour pressure](/wiki/Vapor_pressure "Vapor pressure") assists with cold starts. The *aromatic hydrocarbons* are [unsaturated hydrocarbons](/wiki/Degree_of_unsaturation "Degree of unsaturation") that have one or more [benzene rings](/wiki/Benzene_ring "Benzene ring"). They tend to burn with a sooty flame, and many have a sweet aroma. Some are [carcinogenic](/wiki/Carcinogenic "Carcinogenic").

These different components are separated by [fractional distillation](/wiki/Fractional_distillation "Fractional distillation") at an oil refinery to produce gasoline, jet fuel, kerosene, and other hydrocarbon fractions. The components in an oil sample can be determined by [gas chromatography](/wiki/Gas_chromatography "Gas chromatography") and [mass spectrometry](/wiki/Mass_spectrometry "Mass spectrometry").[[69]](#cite_note-69) Due to the large number of co-[eluted](/wiki/Elution "Elution") hydrocarbons within oil, many cannot be resolved by traditional gas chromatography. This [unresolved complex mixture](/wiki/Unresolved_complex_mixture "Unresolved complex mixture") (UCM) of hydrocarbons is particularly apparent when analysing weathered oils and extracts from tissues of organisms exposed to oil.

Crude oil varies greatly in appearance depending on its composition. It is usually black or dark brown (although it may be yellowish, reddish, or even greenish). In the reservoir it is usually found in association with natural gas (which being lighter forms a "gas cap" over the petroleum) and [saline water](/wiki/Saline_water "Saline water") (which being heavier than most forms of crude oil, generally sinks beneath it). Crude oil may also be found in a semi-solid form mixed with sand and water, as in the [Athabasca oil sands](/wiki/Athabasca_oil_sands "Athabasca oil sands") in Canada, where it is usually referred to as crude bitumen. In Canada, bitumen is considered a sticky, black, tar-like form of crude oil which is so thick and heavy that it must be heated or diluted before it will flow.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Venezuela also has large amounts of oil in the [Orinoco oil sands](/wiki/Orinoco_oil_sands "Orinoco oil sands"), although the hydrocarbons trapped in them are less viscous than in Canada and are usually called extra heavy oil. Oil sands resources are called [unconventional oil](/wiki/Unconventional_oil "Unconventional oil") to distinguish them from oil which can be extracted using traditional oil well methods. Between them, Canada and Venezuela contain an estimated 3.6 trillion barrels (570×10~~^~~9 m3) of bitumen and extra heavy oil, about twice the volume of the world's reserves of conventional oil.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

## Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Formation")]

### Fossil petroleum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Fossil petroleum")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Treibs%26Chlorophyll.png/250px-Treibs%26Chlorophyll.png)](/wiki/File:Treibs%26Chlorophyll.png)

Structure of a vanadium [porphyrin](/wiki/Porphyrin "Porphyrin") compound (left) extracted from petroleum by [Alfred E. Treibs](/wiki/Alfred_E._Treibs "Alfred E. Treibs"), father of [organic geochemistry](/wiki/Organic_geochemistry "Organic geochemistry"). Treibs noted the close structural similarity of this molecule and [chlorophyll a](/wiki/Chlorophyll_a "Chlorophyll a") (right).[[45]](#cite_note-Treibs_1936_Chlorophyll-_und_Häminderivate-45)[[46]](#cite_note-Kvenvolden_2006_retrospective-46)

Petroleum is a [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") derived from [fossilized](/wiki/Fossilized "Fossilized") organic materials, such as [zooplankton](/wiki/Zooplankton "Zooplankton") and [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae").[[46]](#cite_note-Kvenvolden_2006_retrospective-46)[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72) Vast amounts of these remains settled to sea or lake bottoms where they were covered in [stagnant water](/wiki/Water_stagnation "Water stagnation") (water with no [dissolved oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen_saturation "Oxygen saturation")) or [sediments](/wiki/Sediment "Sediment") (such as [mud](/wiki/Mud "Mud") and [silt](/wiki/Silt "Silt")) faster than they could [decompose aerobically](/wiki/Decomposition#Anaerobic_vs_aerobic "Decomposition"). Approximately 1 m below this sediment, water oxygen concentration was low, below 0.1 mg/L, and [anoxic conditions](/wiki/Anoxic_waters "Anoxic waters") existed. Temperatures also remained constant.[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72)

As further layers settled into the sea or lake bed, intense heat and pressure built up in the lower regions. This process caused the organic matter to change, first into a waxy material known as [kerogen](/wiki/Kerogen "Kerogen") (found in various [oil shales](/wiki/Oil_shale "Oil shale") around the world) and then with more heat into liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons via a process known as [catagenesis](/wiki/Catagenesis_(geology) "Catagenesis (geology)"). Formation of petroleum occurs from hydrocarbon [pyrolysis](/wiki/Pyrolysis "Pyrolysis") in a variety of mainly [endothermic](/wiki/Endothermic "Endothermic") reactions at high temperatures or pressures, or both.[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72)[[73]](#cite_note-Braun-1993-73) These phases are described in detail below.

#### Anaerobic decay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Anaerobic decay")]

In the absence of plentiful oxygen, [*aerobic* bacteria](/wiki/Aerobic_organism "Aerobic organism") were prevented from decaying the organic matter after it was buried under a layer of sediment or water. However, [*anaerobic* bacteria](/wiki/Anaerobic_organism "Anaerobic organism") were able to reduce [sulfates](/wiki/Sulfate "Sulfate") and [nitrates](/wiki/Nitrate "Nitrate") among the matter to [H2S](/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide "Hydrogen sulfide") and [N2](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") respectively by using the matter as a source for other reactants. Due to such anaerobic bacteria, at first, this matter began to break apart mostly via [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis"): [polysaccharides](/wiki/Polysaccharide "Polysaccharide") and [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") were hydrolyzed to [simple sugars](/wiki/Simple_sugars "Simple sugars") and [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") respectively. These were further anaerobically [oxidized](/wiki/Oxidized "Oxidized") at an accelerated rate by the [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") of the bacteria: e.g., proteins went through [oxidative deamination](/wiki/Oxidative_deamination "Oxidative deamination") to [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid"), which in turn reacted further to [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia") and [α-keto acids](/wiki/Keto_acid "Keto acid"). [Monosaccharides](/wiki/Monosaccharide "Monosaccharide") in turn ultimately decayed to [CO2](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") and methane. The anaerobic decay products of amino acids, monosaccharides, [phenols](/wiki/Phenols "Phenols") and [aldehydes](/wiki/Aldehyde "Aldehyde") combined into [fulvic acids](/wiki/Fulvic_acid "Fulvic acid"). Fats and [waxes](/wiki/Wax "Wax") were not extensively hydrolyzed under these mild conditions.[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72)

#### Kerogen formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Kerogen formation")]

Some [phenolic compounds](/wiki/Phenols "Phenols") produced from previous reactions worked as [bactericides](/wiki/Bactericide "Bactericide") and the [Actinomycetales](/wiki/Actinomycetales "Actinomycetales") order of bacteria also produced antibiotic compounds (e.g., [streptomycin](/wiki/Streptomycin "Streptomycin")). Thus the action of anaerobic bacteria ceased at about 10 m below the water or sediment. The mixture at this depth contained fulvic acids, unreacted and partially reacted fats and waxes, slightly modified [lignin](/wiki/Lignin "Lignin"), resins and other hydrocarbons.[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72) As more layers of organic matter settled into the sea or lake bed, intense heat and pressure built up in the lower regions.[[73]](#cite_note-Braun-1993-73) As a consequence, compounds of this mixture began to combine in poorly understood ways to kerogen. Combination happened in a similar fashion as [phenol](/wiki/Phenol "Phenol") and [formaldehyde](/wiki/Formaldehyde "Formaldehyde") molecules react to [urea-formaldehyde](/wiki/Urea-formaldehyde "Urea-formaldehyde") resins, but kerogen formation occurred in a more complex manner due to a bigger variety of reactants. The total process of kerogen formation from the beginning of anaerobic decay is called **diagenesis**, a word that means a transformation of materials by dissolution and recombination of their constituents.[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72)

#### Transformation of kerogen into fossil fuels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transformation of kerogen into fossil fuels")]

Kerogen formation continued to a depth of about 1 [km](/wiki/Km "Km") from the Earth's surface where temperatures may reach around 50 [°C](/wiki/%C2%B0C "°C"). Kerogen formation represents a halfway point between organic matter and fossil fuels: kerogen can be exposed to oxygen, oxidize and thus be lost, or it could be buried deeper inside the [Earth's crust](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") and be subjected to conditions which allow it to slowly transform into fossil fuels like petroleum. The latter happened through **catagenesis** in which the reactions were mostly [radical](/wiki/Radical_(chemistry) "Radical (chemistry)") [rearrangements](/wiki/Rearrangement_reaction "Rearrangement reaction") of kerogen. These reactions took thousands to millions of years, and no external reactants were involved. Due to the radical nature of these reactions, kerogen reacted towards two classes of products: those with low H/C ratio ([anthracene](/wiki/Anthracene "Anthracene") or products similar to it) and those with high H/C ratio (methane or products similar to it); i.e., carbon-rich or hydrogen-rich products. Because catagenesis was closed off from external reactants, the resulting composition of the fuel mixture was dependent on the composition of the kerogen via reaction [stoichiometry](/wiki/Stoichiometry "Stoichiometry"). Three types of kerogen exist: type I (algal), II (liptinic) and III (humic), which were formed mainly from algae, plankton and [woody plants](/wiki/Woody_plant "Woody plant") (this term includes trees, shrubs and [lianas](/wiki/Liana "Liana")) respectively.[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72)

Catagenesis was pyrolytic despite the fact that it happened at relatively low temperatures (when compared to commercial pyrolysis plants) of 60 to several hundred °C. Pyrolysis was possible because of the long reaction times involved. Heat for catagenesis came from the decomposition of radioactive materials of the crust, especially [40K](/wiki/Potassium-40 "Potassium-40"), [232Th](/wiki/Thorium-232 "Thorium-232"), [235U](/wiki/Uranium-235 "Uranium-235") and [238U](/wiki/Uranium-238 "Uranium-238"). The heat varied with [geothermal gradient](/wiki/Geothermal_gradient "Geothermal gradient") and was typically 10–30 °C per km of depth from the Earth's surface. Unusual [magma](/wiki/Magma "Magma") intrusions, however, could have created greater localized heating.[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72)

#### Oil window (temperature range)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Oil window (temperature range)")]

Geologists often refer to the temperature range in which oil forms as an *"oil window"*.[[74]](#cite_note-Stanford_Edu-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72) Below the minimum temperature oil remains trapped in the form of kerogen. Above the maximum temperature the oil is converted to natural gas through the process of [thermal cracking](/wiki/Thermal_cracking "Thermal cracking"). Sometimes, oil formed at extreme depths may migrate and become trapped at a much shallower level. The Athabasca oil sands are one example of this.[[72]](#cite_note-Schobert-2013-72)

### Abiogenic petroleum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Abiogenic petroleum")]

An alternative mechanism to the one described above was proposed by Russian scientists in the mid-1850s, the hypothesis of [abiogenic petroleum origin](/wiki/Abiogenic_petroleum_origin "Abiogenic petroleum origin") (petroleum formed by inorganic means), but this is contradicted by geological and geochemical evidence.[[76]](#cite_note-glasby2006-76) Abiogenic sources of oil have been found but never in commercially profitable amounts. "The controversy isn't over whether abiogenic oil reserves exist," said Larry Nation of the American Association of Petroleum Geologists. "The controversy is over how much they contribute to Earth's overall reserves and how much time and effort geologists should devote to seeking them out."[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Structural_Trap_%28Anticlinal%29.svg/250px-Structural_Trap_%28Anticlinal%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Structural_Trap_(Anticlinal).svg)

A [hydrocarbon](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") trap consists of a reservoir rock (yellow) where oil (red) can accumulate, and a caprock (green) that prevents it from egressing.

Three conditions must be present for a [petroleum reservoir](/wiki/Petroleum_reservoir "Petroleum reservoir") to form:

* A [source rock](/wiki/Source_rock "Source rock") rich in hydrocarbon material buried deeply enough for subterranean heat to cook it into oil,
* A porous and [permeable](/wiki/Permeability_(porous_media) "Permeability (porous media)") reservoir rock where it can accumulate,
* A [caprock](/wiki/Caprock "Caprock") (seal) or other mechanism to prevent the oil from escaping to the surface.

Within these reservoirs, fluids will typically organize themselves like a three-layer cake with a layer of water below the oil layer and a layer of gas above it, although the different layers vary in size between reservoirs. Because most hydrocarbons are less dense than rock or water, they often migrate upward through adjacent rock layers until either reaching the surface or becoming trapped within porous rocks (known as reservoirs) by impermeable rocks above. However, the process is influenced by underground water flows, causing oil to migrate hundreds of kilometres horizontally or even short distances downward before becoming trapped in a reservoir. When hydrocarbons are concentrated in a trap, an oil field forms, from which the liquid can be extracted by drilling and pumping.

The reactions that produce oil and natural gas are often modeled as first order breakdown reactions, where hydrocarbons are broken down to oil and natural gas by a set of parallel reactions, and oil eventually breaks down to natural gas by another set of reactions. The latter set is regularly used in [petrochemical](/wiki/Petrochemical "Petrochemical") plants and [oil refineries](/wiki/Oil_refineries "Oil refineries").

Petroleum has mostly been recovered by oil drilling (natural petroleum springs are rare). Drilling is carried out after studies of [structural geology](/wiki/Structural_geology "Structural geology") (at the reservoir scale), [sedimentary basin analysis](/wiki/Sedimentary_basin_analysis "Sedimentary basin analysis"), and [reservoir characterisation](/wiki/Reservoir_modeling "Reservoir modeling") (mainly in terms of the porosity and permeability of geologic reservoir structures).[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) Wells are drilled into oil reservoirs to extract the crude oil. "Natural lift" production methods that rely on the natural reservoir pressure to force the oil to the surface are usually sufficient for a while after reservoirs are first tapped. In some reservoirs, such as in the Middle East, the natural pressure is sufficient over a long time. The natural pressure in most reservoirs, however, eventually dissipates. Then the oil must be extracted using "[artificial lift](/wiki/Artificial_lift "Artificial lift")" means. Over time, these "primary" methods become less effective and "secondary" production methods may be used. A common secondary method is ["waterflood"](/wiki/Water_injection_(oil_production) "Water injection (oil production)") or injection of water into the reservoir to increase pressure and force the oil to the drilled shaft or "wellbore." Eventually "tertiary" or "enhanced" oil recovery methods may be used to increase the oil's flow characteristics by injecting steam, carbon dioxide and other gases or chemicals into the reservoir. In the United States, primary production methods account for less than 40 percent of the oil produced on a daily basis, secondary methods account for about 50 percent, and tertiary recovery the remaining 10 percent. Extracting oil (or "bitumen") from oil/tar sand and oil shale deposits requires mining the sand or shale and heating it in a vessel or [retort](/wiki/Retort "Retort"), or using "in-situ" methods of injecting heated liquids into the deposit and then pumping the liquid back out saturated with oil.

### Unconventional oil reservoirs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Unconventional oil reservoirs")]

See also: [Unconventional oil](/wiki/Unconventional_oil "Unconventional oil"), [Oil shale reserves](/wiki/Oil_shale_reserves "Oil shale reserves"), and [Unconventional (oil and gas) reservoir](/wiki/Unconventional_(oil_and_gas)_reservoir "Unconventional (oil and gas) reservoir")

Oil-eating bacteria [biodegrade](/wiki/Biodegrade "Biodegrade") oil that has escaped to the surface. Oil sands are reservoirs of partially biodegraded oil still in the process of escaping and being biodegraded, but they contain so much migrating oil that, although most of it has escaped, vast amounts are still present—more than can be found in conventional oil reservoirs. The lighter fractions of the crude oil are destroyed first, resulting in reservoirs containing an extremely heavy form of crude oil, called crude bitumen in Canada, or extra-heavy crude oil in Venezuela. These two countries have the world's largest deposits of oil sands.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

On the other hand, oil shales are source rocks that have not been exposed to heat or pressure long enough to convert their trapped hydrocarbons into crude oil. Technically speaking, oil shales are not always shales and do not contain oil, but are fined-grain sedimentary rocks containing an insoluble organic solid called kerogen. The kerogen in the rock can be converted into crude oil using heat and pressure to simulate natural processes. The method has been known for centuries and was patented in 1694 under British Crown Patent No. 330 covering, "A way to extract and make great quantities of pitch, tar, and oil out of a sort of stone." Although oil shales are found in many countries, the United States has the world's largest deposits.[[81]](#cite_note-Lambertson-81)

Some [marker crudes](/wiki/Benchmark_(crude_oil) "Benchmark (crude oil)") with their [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") content (horizontal) and [API gravity](/wiki/API_gravity "API gravity") (vertical) and relative production quantity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [petroleum industry](/wiki/Petroleum_industry "Petroleum industry") generally classifies crude oil by the geographic location it is produced in (e.g., [West Texas Intermediate](/wiki/West_Texas_Intermediate "West Texas Intermediate"), [Brent](/wiki/Brent_oilfield "Brent oilfield"), or [Oman](/wiki/DME_Oman_Crude_Oil_Futures_Contract "DME Oman Crude Oil Futures Contract")), its [API gravity](/wiki/API_gravity "API gravity") (an oil industry measure of density), and its sulfur content. Crude oil may be considered *[light](/wiki/Light_crude_oil "Light crude oil")* if it has low density, *[heavy](/wiki/Heavy_crude_oil "Heavy crude oil")* if it has high density, or *medium* if it has a density between that of *light* and *heavy*.[[82]](#cite_note-82) Additionally, it may be referred to as *[sweet](/wiki/Sweet_crude_oil "Sweet crude oil")* if it contains relatively little sulfur or *[sour](/wiki/Sour_crude_oil "Sour crude oil")* if it contains substantial amounts of sulfur.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

The geographic location is important because it affects transportation costs to the refinery. *Light* crude oil is more desirable than *heavy* oil since it produces a higher yield of gasoline, while *sweet* oil commands a higher price than *sour* oil because it has fewer environmental problems and requires less refining to meet sulfur standards imposed on fuels in consuming countries. Each crude oil has unique molecular characteristics which are revealed by the use of [crude oil assay](/wiki/Crude_oil_assay "Crude oil assay") analysis in petroleum laboratories.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

[Barrels](/wiki/Barrel_(unit) "Barrel (unit)") from an area in which the crude oil's molecular characteristics have been determined and the oil has been classified are used as pricing references throughout the world. Some of the common reference crudes are:[[85]](#cite_note-85)

* [West Texas Intermediate](/wiki/West_Texas_Intermediate "West Texas Intermediate") (WTI), a very high-quality, sweet, light oil delivered at [Cushing, Oklahoma](/wiki/Cushing,_Oklahoma "Cushing, Oklahoma") for North American oil.
* [Brent Blend](/wiki/Brent_Blend "Brent Blend"), consisting of 15 oils from fields in the [Brent](/wiki/Brent_oilfield "Brent oilfield") and [Ninian](/wiki/Ninian_Central_Platform "Ninian Central Platform") systems in the [East Shetland Basin](/wiki/East_Shetland_Basin "East Shetland Basin") of the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"). The oil landed at [Sullom Voe](/wiki/Sullom_Voe "Sullom Voe") terminal in [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland"). Oil production from Europe, Africa and Middle Eastern oil flowing West tends to be priced off this oil, which forms a benchmark.
* [Dubai-Oman](/wiki/Dubai_Crude "Dubai Crude"), used as a benchmark for the Middle East sour crude oil flowing to the Asia-Pacific region.
* [Tapis](/wiki/Tapis_crude "Tapis crude") (from Malaysia, used as a reference for light Far East oil).
* Minas (from Indonesia, used as a reference for heavy Far East oil).
* The [OPEC Reference Basket](/wiki/OPEC_Reference_Basket "OPEC Reference Basket"), a weighted average of oil blends from various [OPEC](/wiki/OPEC "OPEC") (Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries) countries
* [Midway Sunset](/wiki/Midway-Sunset_Oil_Field "Midway-Sunset Oil Field") Heavy, by which heavy oil in California is priced.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]
* [Western Canadian Select](/wiki/Western_Canadian_Select "Western Canadian Select") the benchmark crude oil for emerging heavy, high TAN (acidic) crudes.[[87]](#cite_note-NRCPetroleumProducts-87)

There are declining amounts of these benchmark oils being produced each year, so other oils are more commonly what is actually delivered. While the reference price may be for WTI delivered at Cushing, the actual oil being traded may be a discounted Canadian heavy oil – Western Canadian Select – delivered at [Hardisty](/wiki/Hardisty,_Alberta "Hardisty, Alberta"), Alberta, and for a Brent Blend delivered at Shetland, it may be a discounted Russian Export Blend delivered at the port of [Primorsk](/wiki/Primorsk,_Leningrad_Oblast "Primorsk, Leningrad Oblast").[[88]](#cite_note-88)

## Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Use")]

Once extracted, oil is refined and separated, most easily by [distillation](/wiki/Continuous_distillation#Continuous_distillation_of_crude_oil "Continuous distillation"), into numerous products for direct use or use in manufacturing, such as gasoline (petrol), diesel and kerosene to asphalt and chemical reagents ([ethylene](/wiki/Ethylene "Ethylene"), [propylene](/wiki/Propylene "Propylene"), [butene](/wiki/Butene "Butene"), [acrylic acid](/wiki/Acrylic_acid "Acrylic acid"), [para-xylene](/wiki/Para-xylene "Para-xylene")[[89]](#cite_note-89)) used to make plastics, [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide") and [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceuticals "Pharmaceuticals").[[90]](#cite_note-Dixie_State_College-90) In terms of volume, most petroleum is converted into fuels for combustion engines. In terms of value, petroleum underpins the petrochemical industry, which includes many high value products such as pharmaceuticals and plastics. 84% by volume of the hydrocarbons present in petroleum is converted into fuels

### Fuels and lubricants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Fuels and lubricants")]

Due to its high [energy density](/wiki/Energy_density "Energy density"), easy transportability and [relative abundance](/wiki/Oil_reserves "Oil reserves"), oil has become the world's most important source of energy since the mid-1950s. Petroleum is used mostly, by volume, for refining into [fuel oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") and gasoline, both important *[primary energy](/wiki/Primary_energy "Primary energy")* sources. , including gasoline, diesel, jet, heating, and other fuel oils, and [liquefied petroleum gas](/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas "Liquefied petroleum gas").[[91]](#cite_note-91) Closely related to fuels for combustion engines are [Lubricants](/wiki/Lubricant "Lubricant"), [greases](/wiki/Grease_(lubricant) "Grease (lubricant)"), and [viscosity](/wiki/Viscosity "Viscosity") stabilizers.

### Chemicals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Chemicals")]

Further information: [Petroleum product](/wiki/Petroleum_product "Petroleum product") and [Petrochemical](/wiki/Petrochemical "Petrochemical")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Alkene_General_Formula_V.1.svg/250px-Alkene_General_Formula_V.1.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Alkene_General_Formula_V.1.svg)

General structure of an [alkene](/wiki/Alkene "Alkene")

Many pharmaceuticals are derived from petroleum, albeit via multi-step processes. Modern medicine depends on petroleum as a source of building blocks, reagents, and solvents.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Similarly, virtually all pesticides, insecticides, and herbicides are derived from petroleum. Pesticides have profoundly affected life expectancies by controlling [disease vectors](/wiki/Disease_vector "Disease vector") and by increasing [crop yield](/wiki/Crop_yield "Crop yield"). Like pharmaceuticals, pesticides are in essence petrochemicals. Almost all plastics and synthetic polymers are derived from petroleum, which is the source of monomers. [Alkenes](/wiki/Alkenes "Alkenes") (olefins) are one important class of these precursor molecules.

* [Sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") and its derivative [sulfuric acid](/wiki/Sulfuric_acid "Sulfuric acid"). Hydrogen sulfide is a product of [sulfur removal](/wiki/Hydrodesulfurization "Hydrodesulfurization") from petroleum fraction. It is oxidized to elemental sulfur and then to sulfuric acid.
* Bulk [tar](/wiki/Tar "Tar") and [asphalt](/wiki/Bitumen "Bitumen").
* [Petroleum coke](/wiki/Petroleum_coke "Petroleum coke"), used in speciality carbon products or as solid fuel.

Venezuela and Middle Eastern countries have the largest proven crude oil reserves.[[94]](#cite_note-Petroleum_industry_SciAm_20260107-94)

The [petroleum industry](/wiki/Petroleum_industry "Petroleum industry"), also known as the oil industry, includes the global processes of [exploration](/wiki/Hydrocarbon_exploration "Hydrocarbon exploration"), [extraction](/wiki/Extraction_of_petroleum "Extraction of petroleum"), [refining](/wiki/Oil_refinery "Oil refinery"), [transportation](/wiki/Petroleum_transport "Petroleum transport") (often by [oil tankers](/wiki/Oil_tanker "Oil tanker") and [pipelines](/wiki/Pipeline_transport "Pipeline transport")), and [marketing](/wiki/Downstream_(petroleum_industry)#Marketing "Downstream (petroleum industry)") of [petroleum products](/wiki/List_of_crude_oil_products "List of crude oil products"). The largest volume products of the industry are [fuel oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") and [gasoline](/wiki/Gasoline "Gasoline") (petrol). Petroleum is also the raw material for many [chemical products](/wiki/Petrochemical "Petrochemical"), including [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_drug "Pharmaceutical drug"), [solvents](/wiki/Solvent "Solvent"), [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer"), [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide"), synthetic [fragrances](/wiki/Aroma_compound "Aroma compound"), and [plastics](/wiki/Plastic "Plastic"). The industry is usually divided into three major components: [upstream](/wiki/Upstream_(petroleum_industry) "Upstream (petroleum industry)"), [midstream](/wiki/Midstream "Midstream"), and [downstream](/wiki/Downstream_(petroleum_industry) "Downstream (petroleum industry)"). Upstream regards exploration and extraction of crude oil, midstream encompasses transportation and [storage](/wiki/Oil_terminal "Oil terminal") of it, and downstream concerns refining crude oil into various [end products](/wiki/Petroleum_product "Petroleum product").

Petroleum is vital to many industries, and is necessary for the maintenance of industrial [civilization](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization") in its current configuration, making it a critical concern for many nations. Oil accounts for a large percentage of the world's [energy consumption](/wiki/Energy_consumption "Energy consumption"), ranging from a low of 32% for Europe and Asia, to a high of 53% for the Middle East.

Other geographic regions' consumption patterns are as follows: [South](/wiki/South_America "South America") and [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") (44%), [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") (41%), and [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") (40%). The world consumes 36 billion [barrels](/wiki/Barrel_(unit) "Barrel (unit)") (5.8 km3) of oil per year,[[95]](#cite_note-95) with [developed nations](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") being the largest consumers. The [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") consumed 18% of the oil produced in 2015.[[96]](#cite_note-96) The production, distribution, refining, and retailing of petroleum taken as a whole represents the world's largest industry in terms of dollar value.

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Transport")]

This section is an excerpt from [Petroleum transport](/wiki/Petroleum_transport "Petroleum transport").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Petroleum_transport&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Oil_train_01.jpg/250px-Oil_train_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oil_train_01.jpg)

Oil train near [La Crosse, Wisconsin](/wiki/La_Crosse,_Wisconsin "La Crosse, Wisconsin")

[Petroleum transport](/wiki/Petroleum_transport "Petroleum transport") is the transportation of petroleum and derivatives such as gasoline ([petrol](/wiki/Petrol "Petrol")).[[97]](#cite_note-97) Petroleum products are transported via rail cars, trucks, tanker vessels, and pipeline networks. The method used to move the petroleum products depends on the volume that is being moved and its destination. Land-based transportation modes such as pipelines and rail each have strengths and weaknesses.  One of the key differences is the cost associated with transporting petroleum though pipeline or rail. The risks with moving petroleum products are pollution related and the chance of spillage. Petroleum oil is very hard to clean up and is very toxic to living animals and their surroundings.

In the 1950s, shipping costs made up 33% of the price of oil transported from the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") to the United States,[[98]](#cite_note-economist.com-98) but with the development of [supertankers](/wiki/Oil_tanker "Oil tanker") in the 1970s, the cost of shipping dropped to 5% of the price of Persian oil in the US.[[98]](#cite_note-economist.com-98) The share of the shipping cost on the final cost of the delivered commodity was less than 3% in 2010.

The [price of oil](/wiki/Price_of_oil "Price of oil"), or the oil price, generally refers to the [spot price](/wiki/Spot_price "Spot price") of a [barrel](/wiki/Oil_barrel "Oil barrel") (42 U.S. gallons; 159 liters) of [benchmark crude oil](/wiki/Benchmark_crude_oil "Benchmark crude oil")—a reference price for buyers and sellers of crude oil such as [West Texas Intermediate](/wiki/West_Texas_Intermediate "West Texas Intermediate") (WTI), [Brent Crude](/wiki/Brent_Crude "Brent Crude"), [Dubai Crude](/wiki/Dubai_Crude "Dubai Crude"), [OPEC Reference Basket](/wiki/OPEC_Reference_Basket "OPEC Reference Basket"), [Tapis crude](/wiki/Tapis_crude "Tapis crude"), [Bonny Light](/wiki/Bonny_Light "Bonny Light"), [Urals oil](/wiki/Urals_oil "Urals oil"), [Isthmus](/wiki/Isthmus-34_Light "Isthmus-34 Light"), and [Western Canadian Select](/wiki/Western_Canadian_Select "Western Canadian Select") (WCS) among others.[[99]](#cite_note-Price_of_oil_Benchmarks_2011-99)[[100]](#cite_note-Price_of_oil_pricingdifferences-100) Oil prices are determined by global [supply and demand](/wiki/Supply_and_demand "Supply and demand"), rather than any country's domestic production level, although the [Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries](/wiki/OPEC "OPEC") (OPEC) has considerable influence.[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)

Crude oil is traded as a future on both the [NYMEX](/wiki/NYMEX "NYMEX") and [ICE](/wiki/Intercontinental_Exchange "Intercontinental Exchange") exchanges.[[103]](#cite_note-103) Futures contracts are agreements in which buyers and sellers agree to purchase and deliver specific amounts of physical crude oil on a given date in the future. A contract covers any multiple of 1,000 barrels and can be purchased up to nine years into the future.[[104]](#cite_note-PortaraCQG-104)

### Consumption
