## Environmental effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Environmental effects")]

Main article: [Environmental impact of the petroleum industry](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_the_petroleum_industry "Environmental impact of the petroleum industry")

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Dieselrainbow.jpg/250px-Dieselrainbow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dieselrainbow.jpg)

A diesel fuel spill on a road, with visible [iridescence](/wiki/Iridescence "Iridescence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Carbonate_system_of_seawater.svg/250px-Carbonate_system_of_seawater.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Carbonate_system_of_seawater.svg)

Seawater acidification

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit), about a quarter of annual global [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") is the carbon dioxide from burning petroleum (plus [methane leaks](/wiki/Methane_leaks "Methane leaks") from the industry).[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[a]](#cite_note-119) Along with the burning of coal, petroleum combustion is the largest contributor to the increase in atmospheric CO2.[[119]](#cite_note-120)[[120]](#cite_note-121) Atmospheric CO2 has risen over the last 150 years to current levels of over 415 [ppmv](/wiki/Ppmv "Ppmv"),[[121]](#cite_note-122) from the [180–300 ppmv of the prior 800 thousand years](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide_in_Earth%27s_atmosphere#Concentrations_in_the_geologic_past "Carbon dioxide in Earth's atmosphere").[[122]](#cite_note-123)[[123]](#cite_note-124)[[124]](#cite_note-125) The rise in Arctic temperature has reduced the minimum [Arctic ice pack](/wiki/Arctic_ice_pack "Arctic ice pack") to 4,320,000 km2 (1,670,000 sq mi), a loss of almost half since satellite measurements started in 1979.[[125]](#cite_note-126)

[Ocean acidification](/wiki/Ocean_acidification "Ocean acidification") is the increase in the acidity of the Earth's oceans caused by the uptake of CO2 from the atmosphere.The saturation state of [calcium carbonate](/wiki/Calcium_carbonate "Calcium carbonate") decreases with the uptake of carbon dioxide in the ocean.[[126]](#cite_note-127) This increase in acidity inhibits all marine life—having a greater effect on smaller organisms as well as shelled organisms such as [Pectinoidea](/wiki/Pectinoidea "Pectinoidea").[[127]](#cite_note-128)

### Extraction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Extraction")]

Oil extraction is simply the removal of oil from the reservoir (oil pool). There are many methods on extracting the oil from the reservoirs for example; mechanical shaking,[[128]](#cite_note-129) water-in-oil emulsion, and [specialty chemicals](/wiki/Specialty_chemicals "Specialty chemicals") called [demulsifiers](/wiki/Demulsifiers "Demulsifiers") that separate the oil from water. Oil extraction is costly and often environmentally damaging. Offshore exploration and extraction of oil disturb the surrounding marine environment.[[129]](#cite_note-130)

### Oil spills

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Oil spills")]

Further information: [List of oil spills](/wiki/List_of_oil_spills "List of oil spills")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/2e/Oil-spill.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oil-spill.jpg)

Kelp after an oil spill.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Oil_Slick_in_the_Timor_Sea_September-2009.jpg/250px-Oil_Slick_in_the_Timor_Sea_September-2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oil_Slick_in_the_Timor_Sea_September-2009.jpg)

Oil slick from the [Montara oil spill](/wiki/Montara_oil_spill "Montara oil spill") in the Timor Sea, September 2009.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/PrestigeVolunteersInGaliciaCoast.jpg/250px-PrestigeVolunteersInGaliciaCoast.jpg)](/wiki/File:PrestigeVolunteersInGaliciaCoast.jpg)

Volunteers cleaning up the aftermath of the [Prestige oil spill](/wiki/Prestige_oil_spill "Prestige oil spill").

Crude oil and refined fuel [spills](/wiki/Oil_spill "Oil spill") from tanker ship accidents have damaged natural ecosystems and human livelihoods in Alaska, the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"), the [Galápagos Islands](/wiki/Gal%C3%A1pagos_Islands "Galápagos Islands"), France and many other places. The quantity of oil spilled during accidents has ranged from a few hundred tons to several hundred thousand tons (e.g., [Deepwater Horizon oil spill](/wiki/Deepwater_Horizon_oil_spill "Deepwater Horizon oil spill"), [SS Atlantic Empress](/wiki/SS_Atlantic_Empress "SS Atlantic Empress"), [Amoco Cadiz](/wiki/Amoco_Cadiz "Amoco Cadiz")). Smaller spills have already proven to have a great impact on ecosystems, such as the [*Exxon Valdez* oil spill](/wiki/Exxon_Valdez_oil_spill "Exxon Valdez oil spill").

Oil spills at sea are generally much more damaging than those on land, since they can spread for hundreds of square miles in a thin oil slick which can cover beaches with a thin coating of oil. This can kill sea birds, mammals, shellfish, and other organisms it coats. Oil spills on land are more readily containable if a makeshift earth dam can be rapidly bulldozed around the spill site before most of the oil escapes, and land animals can avoid the oil more easily.

Control of oil spills is difficult, requiring ad hoc methods and often a large amount of manpower. The dropping of bombs and incendiary devices from aircraft on the [SS *Torrey Canyon*](/wiki/SS_Torrey_Canyon "SS Torrey Canyon") wreck produced poor results;[[130]](#cite_note-131) modern techniques would include pumping the oil from the wreck, like in the [*Prestige* oil spill](/wiki/Prestige_oil_spill "Prestige oil spill") or the [*Erika*](/wiki/MV_Erika "MV Erika") oil spill.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

Though crude oil is predominantly composed of various hydrocarbons, certain nitrogen heterocyclic compounds, such as [pyridine](/wiki/Pyridine "Pyridine"), [picoline](/wiki/Picoline "Picoline"), and [quinoline](/wiki/Quinoline "Quinoline") are reported as contaminants associated with crude oil, as well as facilities processing oil shale or coal and have also been found at legacy [wood treatment](/wiki/Creosote "Creosote") sites. These compounds have a very high water solubility and thus tend to dissolve and move with water. Certain naturally occurring bacteria such as *[Micrococcus](/wiki/Micrococcus "Micrococcus")*, *[Arthrobacter](/wiki/Arthrobacter "Arthrobacter")*, and *[Rhodococcus](/wiki/Rhodococcus "Rhodococcus")* have been shown to degrade these contaminants.[[132]](#cite_note-133)

Because petroleum is a naturally occurring substance, its presence in the environment is not necessarily the result of human causes such as accidents and routine activities ([seismic](/wiki/Seismic "Seismic") exploration, [drilling](/wiki/Boring_(earth) "Boring (earth)"), extraction, refining and combustion). Phenomena such as [seeps](/wiki/Seeps "Seeps")[[133]](#cite_note-134) and [tar pits](/wiki/Tar_pit "Tar pit") are examples of areas that petroleum affects without human involvement.

### Tarballs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Tarballs")]

Main article: [Tarball (oil)](/wiki/Tarball_(oil) "Tarball (oil)")

A tarball is a blob of crude oil (not to be confused with [tar](/wiki/Tar "Tar"), which is a human-made product derived from pine trees or refined from petroleum) which has been weathered after floating in the ocean. Tarballs are an aquatic pollutant in most environments, although they can occur naturally, for example in the [Santa Barbara Channel](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Channel "Santa Barbara Channel") of California[[134]](#cite_note-itah-135)[[135]](#cite_note-hostettler-136) or in the Gulf of Mexico off Texas.[[136]](#cite_note-137) Their concentration and features have been used to assess the extent of oil spills. Their composition can be used to identify their sources of origin,[[137]](#cite_note-138)[[138]](#cite_note-139) and tarballs may be dispersed over long distances by deep sea currents.[[135]](#cite_note-hostettler-136) They are slowly decomposed by bacteria, including *[Chromobacterium violaceum](/wiki/Chromobacterium_violaceum "Chromobacterium violaceum")*, *[Cladosporium resinae](/wiki/Cladosporium_resinae "Cladosporium resinae")*, *[Bacillus submarinus](/wiki/Bacillus_submarinus "Bacillus submarinus")*, *[Micrococcus varians](/w/index.php?title=Micrococcus_varians&action=edit&redlink=1 "Micrococcus varians (page does not exist)")*, *[Pseudomonas aeruginosa](/wiki/Pseudomonas_aeruginosa "Pseudomonas aeruginosa")*, *[Candida marina](/wiki/Candida_marina "Candida marina")* and *[Saccharomyces estuari](/w/index.php?title=Saccharomyces_estuari&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saccharomyces estuari (page does not exist)")*.[[134]](#cite_note-itah-135)

### Whales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Whales")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/73/Natural_whale_oil_bottle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Natural_whale_oil_bottle.jpg)

A bottle of unrefined [whale oil](/wiki/Whale_oil "Whale oil")

James S. Robbins has argued that the advent of petroleum-refined kerosene saved some species of great whales from extinction by providing an inexpensive substitute for [whale oil](/wiki/Whale_oil "Whale oil"), thus eliminating the economic imperative for open-boat [whaling](/wiki/Whaling "Whaling"),[[139]](#cite_note-140) but others say that fossil fuels increased whaling with most whales being killed in the 20th century.[[140]](#cite_note-141)

## Alternatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Alternatives")]

In 2018 road transport used 49% of petroleum, aviation 8%, and uses other than energy 17%.[[141]](#cite_note-142) [Electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") are the main alternative for road transport and [biojet](/wiki/Biojet "Biojet") for aviation.[[142]](#cite_note-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144)[[144]](#cite_note-145) [Single-use plastics](/wiki/Disposable_product "Disposable product") have a high carbon footprint and may pollute the sea, but as of 2022 the best alternatives are unclear.[[145]](#cite_note-146)

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: International relations")]

See also: [2022 boycott of Russia and Belarus](/wiki/2022_boycott_of_Russia_and_Belarus "2022 boycott of Russia and Belarus")

Control of petroleum production has been a significant driver of international relations during much of the 20th and 21st centuries.[[146]](#cite_note-The_Economist-2020-147) Organizations like [OPEC](/wiki/OPEC "OPEC") have played an outsized role in international politics. Some historians and commentators have called this the "[Age of Oil](/wiki/Age_of_Oil "Age of Oil")"[[146]](#cite_note-The_Economist-2020-147) With the rise of [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") and addressing climate change some commentators expect a realignment of international power away from petrostates.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Corruption

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Corruption")]

"Oil rents" have been described as connected with corruption in political literature.[[147]](#cite_note-148) A 2011 study suggests that increases in oil rents increased corruption in countries with heavy government involvement in the production of oil. The study found that increases in oil rents "significantly deteriorates political rights". The investigators say that oil exploitation gave politicians "an incentive to extend civil liberties but reduce political rights in the presence of oil windfalls to evade redistribution and conflict".[[148]](#cite_note-149)

### Conflict

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Conflict")]

Main articles: [oil war](/wiki/Oil_war "Oil war") and [Petro-aggression](/wiki/Petro-aggression "Petro-aggression")

Petroleum production has been linked with conflict for many years, leading to thousands of deaths.[[149]](#cite_note-150) Petroleum deposits are in very few countries around the world.[[150]](#cite_note-151)[[151]](#cite_note-152) Conflicts may start when countries refuse to cut oil production in which other countries respond to such actions by increasing their production causing a trade war as experienced during the [2020 Russia–Saudi Arabia oil price war](/wiki/2020_Russia%E2%80%93Saudi_Arabia_oil_price_war "2020 Russia–Saudi Arabia oil price war").[[152]](#cite_note-153) Other conflicts start with countries wanting petroleum resources or other reasons on oil resource territory experienced in the [Iran–Iraq War](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War "Iran–Iraq War").[[153]](#cite_note-154)

### OPEC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: OPEC")]

This section is an excerpt from [OPEC](/wiki/OPEC "OPEC").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=OPEC&action=edit)]

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries ([OPEC](/wiki/OPEC "OPEC") [/ˈoʊpɛk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*OH-pek*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is an organization enabling the co-operation of leading oil-producing and oil-dependent countries in order to collectively influence the global oil market and maximize [profit](/wiki/Profit_(economics) "Profit (economics)"). It was founded on 14 September 1960 in [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad") by the first five members: [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait"), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"). The organization, which currently comprises 12 member countries, accounted for 38 percent of [global oil production](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_oil_production "List of countries by oil production") in 2022.[[154]](#cite_note-OPEC_:4-155)[[155]](#cite_note-156) It is estimated that 79.5 percent of the world's proven oil reserves are located within OPEC nations, with the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") alone accounting for 67.2 percent of OPEC's total reserves.[[156]](#cite_note-OPEC_:52-157)[[157]](#cite_note-OPEC_:62-158)

In a series of steps in the 1960s and 1970s, OPEC restructured the global system of oil production in favor of oil-producing states and away from an [oligopoly](/wiki/Oligopoly "Oligopoly") of dominant Anglo-American oil firms, the "[Seven Sisters](/wiki/Seven_Sisters_(oil_companies) "Seven Sisters (oil companies)")".[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColgan2021The_Rise_of_OPEC,_pp._59–93-159) In the 1970s, [restrictions in oil production](/wiki/1970s_energy_crisis "1970s energy crisis") led to a dramatic rise in oil prices with long-lasting and far-reaching consequences for the global economy. Since the 1980s, OPEC has had a limited impact on world oil-supply and oil-price stability, as there is frequent cheating by members on their commitments to one another, and as member commitments reflect what they would do even in the absence of OPEC.[[159]](#cite_note-OPEC_:1-160)

The formation of OPEC marked a turning point toward [national sovereignty over natural resources](/wiki/Nationalization_of_oil_supplies "Nationalization of oil supplies"). OPEC decisions have come to play a prominent role in the global oil market and in [international relations](/wiki/Petroleum_politics "Petroleum politics"). Economists have characterized OPEC as a textbook example of a [cartel](/wiki/Cartel "Cartel")[[160]](#cite_note-161) (a group whose members cooperate to reduce [market competition](/wiki/Competition_(economics) "Competition (economics)")) but one whose consultations may be protected by the doctrine of [state immunity](/wiki/State_immunity "State immunity") under international law.[[161]](#cite_note-162)

The current OPEC members are[[ref]](http://www.opec.org/opec_web/en/about_us/25.htm) Algeria, Equatorial Guinea, Gabon, Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Libya, Nigeria, the Republic of the Congo, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates and Venezuela. The former members are Angola, Ecuador, Indonesia, and Qatar.[[162]](#cite_note-163) OPEC+ is a larger group consisting of OPEC members and other oil-producing countries. It was formed in late 2016 to better control the global crude oil market.[[163]](#cite_note-OPEC_ope2-164) Canada, Egypt, Norway, and Oman are observer states.

## Future production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Future production")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(February 2021)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/World_oil_production.webp/250px-World_oil_production.webp.png)](/wiki/File:World_oil_production.webp)

World oil production by average barrels per day between 2011 and 2022

[Consumption](/wiki/Consumption_function "Consumption function") in the 20th and 21st centuries has been abundantly pushed by automobile sector growth. The [1985–2003 oil glut](/wiki/1980s_oil_glut "1980s oil glut") even fueled the sales of low fuel economy vehicles in [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") countries. The 2008 economic crisis seems to have had some impact on the sales of such vehicles; still, in 2008 oil consumption showed a small increase.

In 2016 Goldman Sachs predicted lower demand for oil due to emerging economies concerns, especially China.[[164]](#cite_note-165) The [BRICS](/wiki/BRICS "BRICS") (Brasil, Russia, India, China, South Africa) countries might also kick in, as China briefly had the largest automobile market in December 2009.[[165]](#cite_note-166) In the long term, uncertainties linger; the OPEC believes that the OECD countries will push low consumption policies at some point in the future; when that happens, it will definitely curb oil sales, and both OPEC and the [Energy Information Administration](/wiki/Energy_Information_Administration "Energy Information Administration") kept lowering their 2020 consumption estimates during the past five years.[[166]](#cite_note-167) A detailed review of [International Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Energy_Agency "International Energy Agency") oil projections have revealed that revisions of world oil production, price and investments have been motivated by a combination of demand and supply factors.[[167]](#cite_note-Wachtmeister2018-168) All together, non-OPEC conventional projections have been fairly stable the last 15 years, while downward revisions were mainly allocated to OPEC. Upward revisions are primarily a result of US [tight oil](/wiki/Tight_oil "Tight oil").

Production will also face an increasingly complex situation; while OPEC countries still have large reserves at low production prices, newly found reservoirs often lead to higher prices; offshore giants such as [Tupi](/wiki/Tupi_oil_field "Tupi oil field"), Guara and [Tiber](/wiki/Tiber_oilfield "Tiber oilfield") demand high investments and ever-increasing technological abilities. Subsalt reservoirs such as Tupi were unknown in the 20th century, mainly because the industry was unable to probe them. [Enhanced oil recovery](/wiki/Enhanced_Oil_Recovery "Enhanced Oil Recovery") techniques such as those used at the [Daqing Oil Field](/wiki/Daqing_Oil_Field "Daqing Oil Field")[[168]](#cite_note-169) will continue to play a major role in increasing the world's recoverable oil.

The expected availability of petroleum resources has always been around 35 years or even less since the start of the modern exploration. The [oil constant](/wiki/Oil_constant "Oil constant"), an insider pun in the German industry, refers to that effect.[[169]](#cite_note-170) A growing number of divestment campaigns from major funds pushed by newer generations who question the sustainability of petroleum may hinder the financing of future oil prospection and production.[[170]](#cite_note-171)

### Peak oil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Peak oil")]

[Peak oil](/wiki/Peak_oil "Peak oil") is a term applied to the projection that future petroleum production, whether for individual oil wells, entire oil fields, whole countries, or worldwide production, will eventually peak and then decline at a similar rate to the rate of increase before the peak as these reserves are exhausted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[171]](#cite_note-172) The peak of oil discoveries was in 1965, and oil production per year has surpassed oil discoveries every year since 1980.[[172]](#cite_note-173)

Lack of knowledge and/or transparency in the accounting of global oil reserves makes it difficult to predict the oil peak in any given region.[[173]](#cite_note-174) Based on available production data, proponents have previously predicted the peak for the world in 1989, 1995, or 1995–2000. Some of these predictions date from before the recession of the early 1980s and the consequent lowering in global consumption, the effect of which was to delay the date of any peak by several years. Just as the 1971 U.S. peak in oil production was only clearly recognized after the fact, a peak in world production will be difficult to discern until production clearly drops off.[[174]](#cite_note-175) In 2020, according to [BP's Energy Outlook 2020](/wiki/BP#Climate_policy "BP"), peak oil had been reached, due to the changing energy landscape coupled with the [economic toll of the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Financial_market_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic#Oil_prices "Financial market impact of the COVID-19 pandemic").

While there has been much focus historically on peak oil supply, the focus is increasingly shifting to peak demand as more countries seek to transition to renewable energy. The GeGaLo index of geopolitical gains and losses assesses how the geopolitical position of 156 countries may change if the world fully transitions to renewable energy resources. Former oil exporters are expected to lose power, while the positions of former oil importers and countries rich in renewable energy resources is expected to strengthen.[[175]](#cite_note-176)

### Unconventional oil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Unconventional oil")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(May 2022)* |

The calculus for peak oil has changed with the introduction of unconventional production methods. In particular, the combination of horizontal drilling and hydraulic fracturing has resulted in a significant increase in production from previously uneconomic plays.[[176]](#cite_note-177) Certain rock strata contain hydrocarbons but have low permeability and are not thick from a vertical perspective. Conventional vertical wells would be unable to economically retrieve these hydrocarbons. Horizontal drilling, extending horizontally through the strata, permits the well to access a much greater volume of the strata. Hydraulic fracturing creates greater permeability and increases hydrocarbon flow to the wellbore.

## Hydrocarbons on other worlds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petroleum&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Hydrocarbons on other worlds")]

On [Saturn's](/wiki/Saturn%27s "Saturn's") largest moon, [Titan](/wiki/Titan_(moon) "Titan (moon)"), lakes of liquid hydrocarbons comprising methane, ethane, propane and other constituents occur naturally. Data collected by the space probe *[Cassini–Huygens](/wiki/Cassini%E2%80%93Huygens "Cassini–Huygens")* yield an estimate that the visible lakes and seas of Titan contain about 300 times the volume of Earth's proven oil reserves.[[177]](#cite_note-oil-178)[[178]](#cite_note-179) Drilled samples from the surface of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") taken in 2015 by the [*Curiosity* rover's](/wiki/Curiosity_Rover "Curiosity Rover") [Mars Science Laboratory](/wiki/Mars_Science_Laboratory "Mars Science Laboratory") have found organic molecules of benzene and propane in 3-billion-year-old rock samples in [Gale Crater](/wiki/Gale_(crater) "Gale (crater)").[[179]](#cite_note-nyt20180607-180)
